meta.name: description
meta.content: Claude is Anthropic's AI, built for problem solvers. Tackle complex challenges, analyze data, write code, and think through your hardest work.

div.h2: Claude responded: A few common culprits when Django keeps serving old content even though the server restarted cleanly:

div.span: Diagnosed caching culprits and sought clarification on symptoms

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Diagnosed caching culprits and sought clarification on symptoms

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: A few common culprits when Django keeps serving old content even though the server restarted cleanly:

p.br: Hard refresh with Ctrl+Shift+R (or open in an incognito window). Browsers aggressively cache static files.

If you're loading CSS/JS via {% static %} and have run collectstatic before, Django (outside DEBUG mode) or your browser may be serving the old copies from STATIC_ROOT. In development, make sure DEBUG = True in settings so Django serves static files fresh from your app folders instead of the collected copy.

If two apps have a template with the same name (e.g. both have templates/index.html), Django's template loader can pick the wrong app's version depending on INSTALLED_APPS order. Check that your templates are namespaced like app_name/templates/app_name/index.html.
Rare with runserver, but if you were previously running via something else (gunicorn, etc.), stale bytecode can linger. Try:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre-wrap;

div.p: (On Windows, delete __pycache__ folders manually or via a small script.)

p.br: Sounds obvious, but double check you're editing the file that's actually being imported — e.g. editing a template/view in an app that isn't hooked up to the URL you're testing, or having two settings files and the server pointing to a different one than you think.

If "old one" means old data (like an old todo item) rather than old code, that's not a code-reload issue at all — it just means the database still has that row. Check via python manage.py shell or the admin panel.

div.p: Which one matches what you're seeing — old styling/layout, old logic/behavior, or old data?

div.h2: Claude responded: That template itself looks fine — the issue is almost certainly that Django is finding a different about.

div.span: Investigated template loading conflicts and context issues

div.span: Investigated template loading conflicts and context issues

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: That template itself looks fine — the issue is almost certainly that Django is finding a different about.html before it finds this one, or the view isn't passing name into the context.
A few things to check:

p.br: If you have more than one app, and more than one has a templates/about.html (not namespaced under templates/<app_name>/about.html), Django will use whichever one it finds first based on app order in INSTALLED_APPS / template loader search order. Search your whole project for other about.html files:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: If you find more than one, that's your bug — rename/move this one to templates/<yourapp>/about.html and reference it in the view as "<yourapp>/about.html".

p.strong: 2. Check the view is actually passing name
p.br: If name isn't in the context, {{ name }} will just render blank — not "old content" exactly, but worth confirming:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

p.strong: 3. Confirm this file is where Django expects templates
p.br: Is it inside <app>/templates/about.html and does 'APP_DIRS': True exist in your TEMPLATES setting in settings.py? If APP_DIRS is False and you don't have this path listed in DIRS, Django won't even see this file — meaning it's rendering some other about.html it can find (maybe a leftover from earlier in the project).

div.p: Can you tell me what "the old one" looks like — is it completely different HTML, or just missing the {{ name }} value? And what's your project folder structure (app names + where this about.html sits)?

div.p: Yes, that's correct for Windows (Command Prompt or PowerShell).
Just to be precise about the syntax:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

div.p: Note it's Scripts (capital S, plural) not scripts. Windows paths are usually case-insensitive so scripts probably still works, but Scripts is the actual folder name.

After running it, your prompt should show (venv) at the start of the line — like you already had in your first message ((venv) C:\Users\USER\Desktop\TODOS_APP>), so it looks like it's working correctly.
If you're using PowerShell specifically and get an error like "cannot be loaded because running scripts is disabled on this system", you'd need to run:

div.p: then try activating again. But if it's already working for you, you're all set.

div.span: Diagnosed template loading conflict causing variable rendering failure

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now I can see it clearly. Look at the file explorer on the left — you have a top-level templates/about.html sitting directly under TODOS_APP, separate from your todo app folder. That top-level one contains "HOT DJANGO, YOU WILL LIKE IT" — that's the "old one" being served, not the {{ name }} version you showed me earlier (which is presumably inside todo/templates/about.html or similar).
Django is finding that root-level templates/about.html first (or only), so your edits to the other file never show up.

ol.li: Decide which about.html you actually want to use — the root-level one, or one inside the todo app — and remove or edit the other so there's no conflict.
If you want to use the app-based structure (recommended), move your real about.html to:

div.p: Then delete or rename the root-level templates/about.html so it stops shadowing things.
Can you open settings.py and paste the TEMPLATES block? That'll tell us exactly which folder Django is searching first.

div.span: Diagnosed template directory conflict and weighed resolution options

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Got it — that confirms it. Django's DIRS is pointing at that root-level templates folder, so templates/about.html (the one with "HOT DJANGO, YOU WILL LIKE IT") is exactly the file it's serving. It's not old cached content — it's literally a different file than the one you showed me earlier.
So you have two options:

p.strong: Option A — Just edit the file that's actually being used
p.br: Open the about.html at the top level (TODOS_APP/templates/about.html, visible in your explorer) and replace its content with the version you want:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: Save it, refresh the browser — done. No need to touch settings.py or the view.

p.strong: Option B — Move to app-based structure (cleaner for bigger projects)
p.br: Delete the root templates/about.html, create todo/templates/todo/about.html instead, and update your view to render(request, "todo/about.html", {...}).

div.p: For a simple todo app, Option A is simplest — just edit that one file directly since it's the one Django is actually loading. Is that the one you want to keep working with?

div.h2: Claude responded: Here's how to do that.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: relative h-5 flex items-center justify-center shrink-0 overflow-hidden transition-[width,margin-right] duration-150 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.25,0.9,0.3,1)] w-0 -mr-2

div.span: Formulated Django view strategy for task rendering

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Formulated Django view strategy for task rendering

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Here's how to do that.
1. In views.py (in your todo app), add a Home view:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

p.em: (If you already have a Task model with actual saved tasks, use that instead — see the note below.)

div.p: 2. Create the template at todo/templates/todo/home.html:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: 3. Hook it up in urls.py (your app's urls.py, or todo_list/urls.py if that's where your routes live):

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

hr.p: If you have a Task model (check your models.py — you have one open in your editor), you'd query the database instead of hardcoding a list:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

hr.p: And in the template, if your model has a title field for example:

absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

div.p: Good, that gives me exactly what I need. Let's do this step by step.
Replace your current home view (the one returning HttpResponse) with this:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: You can delete the from django.http import HttpResponse import too, since you won't be using HttpResponse anymore (unless something else in your file still needs it).
Your full views.py should now look like:

div.p: Can you paste what's currently in your urls.py? I want to confirm home is already wired up to a URL path before we test it — if it's already there from before, we're good; if not, we'll add it.

div.h2: Claude responded: Perfect — that's already wired up correctly.

div.p: Perfect — that's already wired up correctly. views.home is mapped to the root URL "" with the name 'home', so no changes needed there.

Make sure you've saved:
views.py with the updated home function
home.html in TODOS_APP/templates/home.html

div.p: i opened my browser and got this message after everything. Page not found (404) Request Method: GET Request URL: http://127.0.0.1:8000/ Using the URLconf defined in todo_list.urls, Django tried these URL patterns, in this order: admin/ todo/ The empty path didn’t match any of these. You’re seeing this error because you have DEBUG = True in your Django settings file. Change that to False, and Django will display a standard 404 page.

div.h2: Claude responded: That error tells us exactly what's wrong: your app's urls.

div.span: Investigated URL configuration routing and app inclusion hierarchy

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: That error tells us exactly what's wrong: your app's urls.py (the one with home and about) isn't being included at the root — it's only reachable under todo/.
Your main project file, todo_list/urls.py, currently looks something like this:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: That means your home view is actually live at http://127.0.0.1:8000/todo/, not at http://127.0.0.1:8000/.

p.strong: Option A — Visit the correct URL
p.br: Just go to:

div.p: No code changes needed.

p.strong: Option B — Make the todo app the root (recommended for this classwork)
p.br: Open todo_list/urls.py and change the todo/ prefix to an empty string:

Can you paste your current todo_list/urls.py so I can confirm exactly what to change?

code.span: The `urlpatterns` list routes URLs to views. For more information please see:
1. Add an import: from my_app import views
2. Add a URL to urlpatterns: path('', views.home, name='home')
1. Add an import: from other_app.views import Home
2. Add a URL to urlpatterns: path('', Home.as_view(), name='home')
1. Import the include() function: from django.urls import include, path
2. Add a URL to urlpatterns: path('blog/', include('blog.urls'))

div.p: Interesting — this file already has path("", include("todo.urls")), which is what we want. But two things stand out:

p.strong: 1. This file is missing its closing bracket

div.p: There's no closing ]. If this is really how the file ends, Django would throw a SyntaxError, not a 404 — so either it's cut off in your paste, or the file has more after this that you didn't include. Can you check the very end of the file?

p.strong: 2. This doesn't match the error you showed me
p.br: Your error page said:

span.span: Using the URLconf defined in todo_list.urls, Django tried these URL patterns:

div.p: That means the actual running file still has path('todo/', include('todo.urls')) — not what you just pasted. That's strange.
A couple of possibilities:

ul.li: You edited the file but haven't saved it yet
There are two urls.py files and you're editing/pasting from the wrong one (double check you're in todo_list/urls.py, not some other copy)
The server didn't pick up the change (unlikely with StatReloader, but let's rule it out)

div.h2: Claude responded: Good goal — a polished GitHub profile can genuinely help with job hunting.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: relative h-5 flex items-center justify-center shrink-0 overflow-hidden transition-[width,margin-right] duration-150 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.25,0.9,0.3,1)] w-0 -mr-2

div.span: Devised comprehensive GitHub optimization strategy for professional recruitment appeal

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Devised comprehensive GitHub optimization strategy for professional recruitment appeal

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Good goal — a polished GitHub profile can genuinely help with job hunting. Here's how to set it up properly, step by step.

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Real name (or a name you'd want a recruiter to see)
Profile photo — clear headshot, not a meme or blank avatar
Bio: one line — e.g. "Backend Developer | Python & Django | Building web apps"
Location, and a link to your portfolio/LinkedIn if you have one

p.br: On your GitHub profile, go to Customize your pins and choose 4–6 of your strongest projects (this todo app can be one, once it's more complete). Avoid pinning tutorial-following repos with no personal changes — pin things that show real work.

p.strong: 3. Every pinned repo needs a good README
p.br: Each project repo should have a README.md with:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: What the project does (1–2 sentences)
Tech stack used (Django, Python, SQLite, etc.)
How to run it locally (setup instructions)
Screenshots if it has a UI
What you learned / challenges solved (optional but strong)

p.strong: 4. Create a Profile README (this is the big one)
p.br: GitHub lets you make a special repo named exactly your username (e.g. Pablo/Pablo) — it displays as a bio page at the top of your profile. This is what recruiters see first. Typically includes:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Short intro
Skills/tech stack (often as badges)
Current projects
How to reach you (email, LinkedIn)

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Meaningful commit messages ("Add task deletion feature" not "update" or "fix")
Regular small commits rather than one giant dump
No .env, db.sqlite3, or venv/ committed — add a proper .gitignore

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Green squares matter less than people think, but a .gitignore-clean, well-documented repo history matters a lot

div.p: Want me to write your Profile README and a .gitignore for this Django project right now? I can also draft a README for the todo app itself once we're further along — that'd be a strong first pinned project.

div.p: Thanks, this gives me a clear picture. Your GitHub username is chizzypablo499-cyber — that's the first thing I'd fix, since recruiters judge fast and a username like that reads as a throwaway/test account rather than a professional developer's account.

p.br: GitHub lets you rename your account (Settings → Account → Change username). Go for something clean and professional:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

ul.li: pablo-nwosu (or whatever your real name is)
Just your real name if available: pablonwosu

div.p: Avoid "cyber," numbers, or anything that looks auto-generated. Recruiters often click straight through from a resume link, and the URL itself is part of the first impression.

p.br: I can see repos like:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

div.p: These are fine to have (learning is normal), but they shouldn't be the ones a recruiter sees first. Two options:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Rename them to something descriptive (list-dictionary-assignment → python-data-structures-practice)
Or make them private if they're just throwaway coursework, and keep public repos limited to polished, presentable work

p.br: Go to your profile page → Customize your pins → choose your 4–6 strongest, cleanly-named repos (this Django todo app is a good candidate once finished).
This is the single highest-impact thing you can do. Want me to draft one for you now? I'll need to know:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Your real name (or preferred professional name)
Your target role (e.g. Backend Developer, Full-Stack, Python Developer)
Key skills so far (Python, Django, etc.)
Any way you want people to contact you (email, LinkedIn)

div.p: Want to start with the username change, or should I write the Profile README first while you think about the rename?

Q: What should I list as your current skills? A: Python + Django + HTML/CSS/JS
Q: How should people contact you? A: Email only

div.h2: Claude responded: Here's your Profile README, ready to go.

div.span: Bypassed formatting protocol to expedite file creation

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Here's your Profile README, ready to go.

p.strong: How to make it show up on your GitHub profile:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: On GitHub, click + (top right) → New repository
Name the repo exactly your username: dev-billions
Make it Public
Check "Add a README file" (or just create it empty — you'll paste content next)
Once created, click the pencil/edit icon on the README and paste in the content from the file I gave you
Commit changes

div.p: GitHub automatically detects a repo with your exact username and displays its README at the top of your profile page.

p.strong: A couple of things to fix before it's fully accurate:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: The GitHub Stats badge and the "Featured Projects" link both assume your todo app repo is named todo-app under dev-billions — update the link once you know the actual repo name
Add more projects to the "Featured Projects" section as you build them

div.p: Want me to also write a proper README.md for the todo app itself next, so it's ready to pin?

div.h2: Claude responded: Great — next step is getting the README content into it.

div.p: Great — next step is getting the README content into it.

p.strong: Fastest way (through GitHub's website, no terminal needed):

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to your new repo: github.com/dev-billions/dev-billions
Click on the README.md file inside it
Click the pencil ✏️ (edit) icon in the top right of the file view
Delete whatever's in there and paste in the full content from the file I generated above
Scroll down, click Commit changes

div.p: Once that's done, go to github.com/dev-billions (your main profile) — the README should now appear right at the top.
Let me know once you've done that, or if you'd rather do it through your local editor/terminal (clone the repo, edit, push) — I can walk you through that instead if you prefer.

div.h2: Claude responded: This looks great visually — but there's one important issue: the URL shows github.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: relative h-5 flex items-center justify-center shrink-0 overflow-hidden transition-[width,margin-right] duration-150 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.25,0.9,0.3,1)] w-0 -mr-2

div.span: Identified repository mismatch preventing code visibility

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Identified repository mismatch preventing code visibility

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: This looks great visually — but there's one important issue: the URL shows github.com/dev-billions/pythonproject/blob/main/readme.md.

That means you added this README to a repo called pythonproject, not to the special profile repo named exactly dev-billions. GitHub only auto-displays a README on your profile page if it lives in a repo whose name exactly matches your username.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to github.com/dev-billions (your profile) and check — did you actually create a repo called dev-billions, or did you create pythonproject instead?

If the repo is named pythonproject:
Go to that repo's Settings
Under "Repository name," rename it to dev-billions
Save
OR, if you'd rather keep pythonproject as an actual project repo (not your profile), create a new, separate repo named exactly dev-billions and move this README content there instead.
Once the README is sitting in a repo named exactly dev-billions, visit github.com/dev-billions again — it should now show up as your profile page content automatically.

div.p: Can you check what repos currently exist under your account and let me know if pythonproject was meant to be your profile repo, or a separate project?

div.h2: Claude responded: Here's the simplest path — renaming your existing pythonproject repo to dev-billions.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.span: Architected sequential instructions for repository renaming

div.span: Architected sequential instructions for repository renaming

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Here's the simplest path — renaming your existing pythonproject repo to dev-billions.

p.br: Open github.com/dev-billions/pythonproject in your browser.
Click the Settings tab (top row of the repo, next to "Code," "Issues," "Pull requests," etc. — you may need to scroll the tab bar or it's near the far right).
Right at the top of the General settings page, there's a field labeled Repository name with your current name pythonproject in it.

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Clear the field
Type exactly: dev-billions
Click the Rename button next to it

p.br: GitHub will rename the repo. Your README should now live at github.com/dev-billions/dev-billions.
Go to github.com/dev-billions (just your username, no repo name after it). Your README content should now be displaying automatically at the top of your profile page.

hr.p: One thing to flag: if pythonproject had other files in it besides the README (actual project code), renaming it turns your profile repo and that project into the same repo — which isn't ideal long-term.
Does pythonproject contain anything besides the README right now, or is it empty aside from that file?

div.h2: Claude responded: I see what happened — the rename didn't actually go through, the URL is still github.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: relative h-5 flex items-center justify-center shrink-0 overflow-hidden transition-[width,margin-right] duration-150 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.25,0.9,0.3,1)] w-0 -mr-2

div.span: Pivoted strategy to establish dedicated repository instead

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Pivoted strategy to establish dedicated repository instead

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: I see what happened — the rename didn't actually go through, the URL is still github.com/dev-billions/pythonproject. It's still just showing the README file content because you're viewing that file directly, not because the repo was renamed.

Also — now that I can see the full repo, it's a good thing the rename didn't stick. This repo already has other files in it (assignment3.py, omega, palindrome.py, study), so turning this into your profile repo would mix your special README with unrelated coursework files. Better to keep this repo as-is and create a new, separate repo just for the profile README.

p.strong: Step-by-step: Create the correct profile repo

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to github.com/new (or click the + icon top-right → New repository)
Repository name: type exactly dev-billions
Set it to Public
Check the box "Add a README file"
Click Create repository

div.p: GitHub will likely show a message like "dev-billions/dev-billions is a special repository" — that confirms you're doing it right.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Once created, click on the README.md file inside this new repo
Click the pencil ✏️ edit icon
Delete the placeholder content and paste in the full README content I gave you earlier
Scroll down and click Commit changes
Then go to github.com/dev-billions (your profile, no repo name) — it should now show the README automatically.

div.p: Want me to also help you rename those messy filenames in pythonproject (assignment3.py, omega, study) so that repo looks more professional too, once the profile one is sorted?

div.h2: Claude responded: Good — let's clean up pythonproject.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.class: relative h-5 flex items-center justify-center shrink-0 overflow-hidden transition-[width,margin-right] duration-150 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.25,0.9,0.3,1)] w-0 -mr-2

div.span: Devised file renaming strategy for project repository

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Devised file renaming strategy for project repository

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Good — let's clean up pythonproject. Based on what's visible in your screenshot, here's what I'd rename each file to (professional, descriptive names help recruiters skim your repo instantly):

th.class: text-text-100 border-b-0.5 border-[hsl(var(--border-300)/0.6)] py-2 pr-4 align-top font-bold
text-text-100 border-b-0.5 border-[hsl(var(--border-300)/0.6)] py-2 pr-4 align-top font-bold

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

td.em: (or a more specific name based on what it does)

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

td.em: (needs a proper .py extension too — right now it has none)

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]
bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

td.em: (if it's notes) or delete if it's just scratch work

p.strong: How to rename a file on GitHub (step by step):

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Open the repo: github.com/dev-billions/pythonproject
Click on the file you want to rename (e.g. assignment3.py)
Click the pencil ✏️ edit icon (top right of the file view)
At the top, there's a field showing the current filename — click into it and type the new name
Scroll down, add a commit message like Rename assignment3.py to class_a_project.py
Click Commit changes

div.p: Repeat for each file.

p.strong: Before we go further, a couple of questions:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: What does omega actually contain? (It has no file extension — is it Python code, notes, or something else?)
What does study contain — is it worth keeping, or is it just scratch/practice you don't need public?

div.p: Once I know that, I can give you exact rename suggestions and also help you add a proper README.md inside this repo (different from your profile one) explaining what each script does — that's what really makes a repo look professional to a recruiter skimming your projects.

The user prompt is empty, so I cannot determine the primary language. However, based on the thinking block being in English, here is my summary:
Reconciled repository naming conflict and weighed consolidation options

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Ah, this explains a lot. This repo chizzypablo499-cyber is a leftover special profile repo from your old username (before you renamed to dev-billions). It already has a README with real bio content — "Chisom Victory," Techrise program, skills — which looks like something you (or a template) set up earlier.

Since your username changed, this repo name no longer matches, so it won't auto-display on your profile anymore. You've now got two competing README setups floating around. Let's consolidate into one clean repo.

p.strong: Best fix: rename this existing repo to dev-billions

div.p: This is better than creating a brand new empty one, since this repo already has real, personal content in it.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to this repo: github.com/dev-billions/chizzypablo499-cyber
Click Settings (top tab row)
At the top, find Repository name
Clear it and type exactly: dev-billions
Click Rename
Then go to github.com/dev-billions (your profile, no repo name after it) and confirm the README now shows up there.

div.p: After that, quick question: did you also create a separate new repo named dev-billions earlier when I walked you through that? If so, we'll have two repos both trying to claim that name, and GitHub won't allow the rename until the conflicting one is deleted or renamed. Can you check github.com/dev-billions?tab=repositories and tell me the full list of repos you currently have?

div.p: Good news: you already have a repo named exactly dev-billions — that's the one GitHub will use for your profile page. The rename isn't even needed anymore.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to github.com/dev-billions (no ?tab=repositories, just your plain profile URL)
Check if the README content is showing at the top

div.p: If it's showing the version I drafted for you (with "Hi, I'm Dev Billions" etc.) — great, that part's working.

p.strong: Now let's clean up the duplicate/clutter:

div.p: You have two competing bio repos:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: dev-billions — has my drafted README
chizzypablo499-cyber — has an older README with your real bio ("Ezinna Chisom," Techrise Program, etc.)

div.p: Since dev-billions is the one that actually displays on your profile, that old chizzypablo499-cyber repo is now redundant and just clutters your repo list.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to github.com/dev-billions/chizzypablo499-cyber
Click Settings
Scroll all the way to the bottom — Danger Zone
Click Delete this repository
Type the repo name to confirm, then delete

div.p: One important question first though: that old README had real, personal details — "Techrise Program," your real name "Ezinna Chisom." Do you want me to merge some of that into your dev-billions README (real name + Techrise program mention), or keep it purely as "Dev Billions" like we set up? Recruiters generally want to see a real name somewhere, so I'd lean toward blending the two — what do you think?

div.p: Happy to help — but first, what exactly do you mean by "my picture"? A few possibilities:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Your GitHub profile photo (the headshot on your account) — I can give you guidance on cropping/style/background for a professional look, but I can't directly edit a personal photo of you.
A banner/graphic for your GitHub profile README — I can design this (e.g. a header image with your name, role, and tech stack) since it's a graphic, not a photo of you.
Something else — like a logo, icon, or project screenshot?

div.p: Which one did you have in mind?

div.h2: Claude responded: I can see the photo — nice, warm smile, decent lighting on your face.

div.span: Charted guidance for professional photo enhancement techniques

span.class: inline-flex -rotate-90 shrink-0 opacity-0 transition-opacity duration-200 group-hover/status:opacity-100 group-focus-visible/status:opacity-100

div.span: Charted guidance for professional photo enhancement techniques

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: I can see the photo — nice, warm smile, decent lighting on your face. But you're right that it's not ideal for a professional profile picture as-is: the background is a busy event banner, the shot is full-body/wide (a profile photo should be head-and-shoulders), and it's crowded with distracting text and background clutter.
I don't have a tool to directly edit or generate a version of your actual photo — but here's exactly how to fix it yourself, step by step, using free tools.

p.br: A profile photo should be a close crop — head + shoulders, face taking up roughly 60% of the frame, centered. Full-body shots don't work well as small circular avatars.

p.strong: Step 2: Blur or replace the background
p.br: Use one of these free tools:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: remove.bg — uploads your photo, automatically removes the background, gives you a transparent PNG
Canva (free account) — has a "Background Remover" tool, then you can add a plain, soft color background (navy blue, light gray, or off-white work great for tech/dev profiles)
Unscreen / Photoroom — similar background removal, mobile-friendly apps

p.br: Once the busy banner is removed, use:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Solid neutral color (charcoal gray, navy, or soft blue)
Or a very light, out-of-focus blur if you want to keep a real background feel

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Straighten the crop so your eyes are roughly level in the frame
Make sure lighting on your face stays even (yours already looks fine)
Export as a square image (GitHub crops to a circle, so keep your face centered)

div.p: Want me to instead design a separate graphic banner for the top of your GitHub README (with your name, role, and tech stack, not your actual photo)? That's something I can build directly as an SVG/HTML graphic.

div.p: That's your GitHub banner. Here's how to use it:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Right-click the banner above → Save image as → save it as banner.svg or convert it to PNG
In your dev-billions repo, create a folder or just upload the image file directly (drag-and-drop into the repo works too)
In your README.md, add this line at the very top:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: (swap the filename if you saved it differently)

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Commit changes — the banner will now appear at the top of your profile, above the "Hi, I'm Dev Billions" heading.

div.p: For your actual profile photo: stick with the crop-and-clean-background approach I described earlier — a real headshot still matters for LinkedIn and recruiter trust, even with a nice banner graphic on GitHub.
Want me to tweak the banner's colors, wording, or add your GitHub username handle to it?

iframe.title: visualize: Dev billions github banner v2
iframe.style: width: 100%; border-width: medium; border-style: none; border-color: currentcolor; border-image: none; background-color: transparent; height: 230px;

p.strong: Step-by-step: Upload the banner and add it to your README

p.br: Right-click the banner above → Save image as → save it to your computer as banner.svg (keep that exact filename, it makes step 3 simpler).

p.strong: Step 2: Go to your profile repo
p.br: Open github.com/dev-billions/dev-billions in your browser.

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Click Add file (top right, near the green "Code" button) → Upload files
Drag banner.svg into the upload box, or click to browse and select it
Scroll down, add a commit message like Add profile banner
Click Commit changes

p.strong: Step 4: Reference it in your README

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Click on README.md in the repo
Click the pencil ✏️ edit icon
At the very top of the file (before "Hi, I'm Dev Billions"), add this line:

ul.li: Scroll down, commit the change
Go to github.com/dev-billions and confirm the banner now shows above your bio text

div.p: Let me know once it's up, or if the image doesn't render (SVG uploads sometimes need a moment, or a PNG export works as a fallback if SVG gives you trouble).
